nativeRange.habitat: Clear, fast-flowing, well-oxygenated mountain streams with rocky substrate, abundant aquatic vegetation, and leaf litter; cooler hill and mountain habitats typically above 300 m elevation

decorPreferences: dense planting (java moss, hornwort, elodea, floating plants) for spawning cover
driftwood and leaf litter for natural biotope feel

diet.notes: Accepts virtually all standard aquarium foods: quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and small granules form the staple. Regular frozen or live foods (daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, mosquito larvae) enhance coloration and condition, particularly for breeding. Grazes on algae and biofilm in the wild; supplement with spirulina-based foods. Small mouth size limits food particle size — crush flakes if necessary.

avoidWith: goldfish (predation risk, goldfish outcompete for food)

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species photo of Tanichthys albonubes showing characteristic red-edged lateral stripe and neon body band.

summary: A hardy, vibrantly coloured cool-water schooling minnow from southern China's White Cloud Mountain. Dubbed the 'Poor Man's Neon' for its striking red-and-gold lateral stripe, it thrives without a heater (16–24°C) and breeds readily in a well-planted tank. Critically Endangered in the wild; rediscovered in Vietnam in 2007 after a decades-long presumed extinction.
careNotes: The White Cloud Mountain Minnow is one of the most beginner-friendly freshwater fish and one of very few true cool-water schooling species suitable for unheated indoor aquariums. A school of at least six is recommended to reduce stress and elicit natural schooling behaviour and full colouration. Males are slimmer with more vivid coloration; females are rounder when in condition. The species thrives in the 16–24°C range and becomes lethargic or susceptible to disease at sustained temperatures above 26°C — do not house with most tropical species requiring heaters set above that range. Strong filtration and good water movement are appreciated, reflecting the species' origin in fast-flowing mountain streams; ensure adequate oxygenation. The 'Golden' variety lacks the lateral stripe pigmentation and shows a yellow-gold body; the 'Long-fin' (Veil-fin) variety has elongated fins and is slightly more delicate; the 'Meteor Minnow' is a trade name referring to a selectively-bred long-fin golden variant. All varieties share the same care requirements. Taxonomy note: some authorities place this species in the monotypic family Tanichthyidae, though Cyprinidae placement remains most broadly accepted; both names appear in literature. Conservation note: the species is listed as Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List; rediscovered in Vietnam (Quang Ninh Province) in 2007 after being thought extinct in the wild for decades; wild populations in China's Guangdong province remain severely depleted due to habitat loss.
breedingNotes: Among the easiest aquarium fish to breed. Condition adults with frequent small live/frozen food feedings. Spawning occurs over fine-leaved plants and moss; eggs are laid individually and parents show little interest in eating them if plants provide cover (unlike many egg scatterers). A dedicated 20–30 L breeding tank with java moss or a fine-leaved plant, no substrate (for egg collection), and gentle filtration is ideal. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 20°C; fry are free-swimming within days and accept infusoria, vinegar eels, or powdered fry food initially, then newly hatched brine shrimp. Multiple broods possible per month from conditioned pairs.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Tanichthys albonubes
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (White Cloud Mountain, Guangdong, China; Vietnam rediscovery 2007), temperature range 14–22°C (operational range widened to 16–24°C per Aquarium Co-Op and FishBase consensus), pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 18–179 ppm (~1–10 dH), adult size to 4 cm, diet (omnivore — algae, small invertebrates), breeding behaviour, and conservation status (Critically Endangered, IUCN 2011 assessment). Taxonomy note: SeriouslyFish places within Cyprinidae; Tanichthyidae treatment also cited in some systematic works but Cyprinidae used here as predominant authority.

additional.name: FishBase — Tanichthys albonubes
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4.5 cm TL max), temperature range (16–22°C), native range (China, Vietnam), taxonomy (family Cyprinidae, order Cypriniformes). FishBase lists family as Cyprinidae. Maximum size 4.5 cm TL noted; operational adult range 3–4 cm used as typical aquarium size.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — White Cloud Mountain Minnow Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide. Confirmed temperature range 60–72°F (15–22°C), tank size recommendation (10 gal / ~40 L minimum), schooling group of 6+, omnivore feeding regime, beginner care level. Noted Golden, Long-fin, and Meteor Minnow variety names.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — White Cloud Mountain Minnow
additional.notes: Confirmed cool-water unheated suitability, 'Poor Man's Neon' origin of nickname (brilliant lateral stripe at fraction of neon tetra cost/fragility), critical endangered status and Vietnam 2007 rediscovery (Luu Viet Hung et al.), breeding ease, and variety overview (Golden, Long-fin).

additional.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist Magazine — White Cloud Mountain Minnow profile
additional.notes: Historical and aquarium-hobby context: species first described 1932 by Lin Shu-Yen based on specimens collected by Boy Scout Tan (genus named Tanichthys in his honour). Confirmed taxonomy debate between Cyprinidae and Tanichthyidae placements; Cyprinidae used as primary per most recent molecular studies cited in FishBase. Notes on Meteor Minnow as trade variant of long-fin golden selectively bred form.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawns readily over java moss or fine-leaved plants; eggs ignored by adults if plant cover is adequate. Fry accept fine powdered foods and newly hatched brine shrimp. No specialised setup required beyond a well-planted dedicated breeding vessel.
